description: The third-person singular pronoun 'shia' refers to one being or thing other than the speaker or addressee. It marks neither gender nor animacy, so English may render it as singular they, he, she, or it. Plural reference adds 'lo' (PL): 'lo shia'.
articulatory_notes: Raise the tongue blade just behind the alveolar ridge for unvoiced 'sh'. A narrow channel remains for air. The tongue then rises and moves forward for unrounded 'i'; the jaw opens onto a separate unstressed 'a'. Stress stays with 'shi'.
sound_symbolism: The same two-vowel cadence runs through all three core pronouns, so they are easy to hear as kin. The opening hush gives 'shia' a quieter beginning.
usage_notes: The form needs no case ending; word order shows whether 'shia' is doing or receiving the action. It also follows prepositions and precedes a noun it possesses. At clause opening it is the subject, though an established topic may be left unspoken.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The pronoun lets a speaker refer to someone without supplying a grammatical gender. The person's own self-description remains theirs.
